title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary of the Paper:**  
This paper examines the "label trick," a technique enabling parallel propagation of labels and features in GNNs. While prior work demonstrated its empirical effectiveness, this study provides a rigorous theoretical analysis. The authors introduce a self-excluded simplification of the label trick via extreme splitting and show that random splits act as regularization for self-excluded label weights. The paper further explores broader applications, including (a) trainable variants of label propagation and Correct & Smooth (C&S), and (b) deterministic implementations for linear-propagation GNNs to eliminate randomness. Experiments validate these extensions on benchmarks like Cora, PubMed, and OGB datasets.  

**Critique:**  
**(1) Empirical Impact of Novel Applications:**  
The paper’s core strength lies in its theoretical grounding of the label trick, which addresses a significant gap by deriving an interpretable deterministic objective (under linearity and MSE assumptions) that resolves label leakage and introduces graph-adaptive regularization. This formalization is a valuable contribution, as highlighted across reviews. However, the novel use-cases (Sec. 4)—trainable label propagation, deterministic GNN variants, and end-to-end trainable C&S—yield marginal empirical gains. Tables 1–4 reveal that performance improvements are often negligible or inconsistent when accounting for standard deviations. For example, trainable C&S shows gains on Cora-full/PubMed but marginal results on ArXiv/Products, and deterministic variants (e.g., for SGC/SIGN) lack comprehensive benchmarking against stochastic counterparts. While the extensions are well-motivated by the theory, their practical impact remains limited, and comparisons to recent strong baselines (e.g., GLEM) are absent.  

**(2) Incomplete Analysis of the Label Trick’s Role:**  
The paper justifiably notes the label trick’s prevalence in top competition submissions (e.g., KDDCUP 2021). However, it does not empirically dissect *why* the technique is effective beyond accuracy metrics. As raised in the main review, an ablation study comparing models with/without the label trick is essential to isolate its contribution—whether it primarily enhances accuracy, accelerates convergence, improves training stability, or mitigates overfitting. The theoretical analysis elegantly links the trick to regularization (e.g., via the Γ term in Eq. 9), but this remains confined to linear models. Extensions to nonlinear GNNs (Sec. 3.4) are underdeveloped, and practical tuning of regularization parameters (e.g., α) is underexplored. Further, scalability concerns for deterministic variants on large-scale graphs (e.g., OGB-Products) are not addressed, limiting the work’s applicability.  

**(3) Technical Refinement and Scope:**  
The analysis relies heavily on simplifying assumptions (linearity, MSE loss), which restricts direct generalizability to complex, nonlinear GNNs. While the deterministic equivalence is insightful, its real-world utility hinges on broader validation across deeper architectures, which is lacking. Notation inconsistencies (e.g., undefined Γ in Sec. 3.3) and insufficient discussion of limitations—such as potential over-smoothing from the regularization term or sensitivity to graph structure—weaken the presentation. Additionally, while the leakage-resistant data-fitting term (Eq. 6) is theoretically elegant, its efficacy in nonlinear settings requires further empirical justification.  

**Recommendation:**  
This work makes a valuable theoretical contribution by formalizing the label trick’s mechanics and proposing innovative extensions. However, the empirical benefits of these extensions are weak, the analysis of the trick’s real-world role is incomplete, and technical gaps (e.g., scalability, nonlinear generalization) remain unaddressed. **I recommend acceptance as is, but only marginally above the threshold (Rating: 6)**. For final publication, the authors should:  
1. **Clarify the label trick’s practical advantages** via ablation studies (e.g., convergence speed, robustness), not just accuracy.  
2. **Strengthen empirical validation** of novel use-cases (e.g., scalability of deterministic variants, deeper GNN benchmarks).  
3. **Address technical gaps**, including notation consistency and limitations of linear assumptions.  
4. **Discuss broader impacts**, such as overfitting risks in trainable C&S or sensitivity of regularization to graph properties.  

**Summary:**  
The paper provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for the label trick and proposes novel applications. While the theory is insightful, the empirical gains from extensions are marginal, and key questions about the trick’s practical utility remain unresolved. With revisions addressing these gaps, the work can better leverage its theoretical contributions.

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary of the Paper:**  
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification for the recently emerging and heuristically proposed "label trick," which involves stochastically incorporating a random portion of node labels in the training set as input node attributes. Starting from the simplest case, the authors derive a general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer for both regression and classification tasks. Motivated by this analysis, they propose novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios, including trainable label propagation, deterministic variants for GNNs with linear propagation layers (e.g., SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS), and end-to-end trainable Correct and Smooth (C&S).  

**Main Review:**  
This work offers a **solid and inspiring theoretical foundation** for the empirically successful but previously heuristic label trick technique. The authors' key contribution lies in deriving a **deterministic equivalent** of the stochastic process under simplifying assumptions (linear models, MSE loss), revealing an interpretable objective comprising a **leakage-resistant data-fitting term** and a **structure-aware regularization term**. This analysis provides valuable insights into the technique's implicit regularization effects and mitigation of label leakage, addressing a significant gap in the literature given the label trick's prevalence in top OGB submissions.  

The derivation is presented smoothly, with **insightful remarks** on the role of the splitting probability (α) and regularization effects. While the theoretical results are rigorous and elegantly resolve leakage in Eq. 6, they **rely on strong linearity assumptions**, limiting direct applicability to complex nonlinear GNNs. The extension to nonlinear models in Sec 3.4 remains preliminary and warrants further exploration—such as approximating the regularization term explicitly for nonlinear losses.  

The **novel use cases** proposed—trainable label propagation, deterministic label trick variants for linear-GNNs, and end-to-end trainable C&S—are **well-motivated by the theoretical grounding** and demonstrate **promising empirical results** across benchmarks (Cora, PubMed, OGB-ArXiv, OGB-Products). Experiments validate performance gains (e.g., +1-2% accuracy) and confirm the practical utility of the deterministic variants. However, the experimental scope could be strengthened:  
1. **Broader architectural validation** is needed, particularly for deeper GNNs beyond linear/two-layer models.  
2. **Mixed results for trainable C&S** (notable gains on Cora-full/PubMed but marginal on ArXiv/Products) suggest further investigation into failure modes or graph-structure dependencies.  
3. Comparisons to **recent strong baselines** (e.g., GLEM) and a comprehensive benchmark of deterministic (D) versus stochastic (S) variants are lacking.  

**Practical considerations** also merit attention. The **scalability** of the deterministic variant for billion-edge graphs (e.g., OGB-Products) requires discussion, including runtime/memory overhead. Additionally, potential **overfitting risks** in trainable C&S and sensitivity of the regularization term (Γ) to graph properties beyond connectivity/size should be addressed. Minor **technical oversights**, such as inconsistent notation (e.g., undefined Γ in Sec 3.3), could be clarified for rigor.  

**Conclusion:**  
Despite limitations imposed by linear assumptions and experimental scope, this work makes **significant contributions** by demystifying a widely adopted technique and proposing innovative, empirically validated extensions. The theoretical analysis is **coherent and impactful**, while the novel applications open promising research avenues. With its **broader implications** for GNN design and feature/label integration, this paper is a **valuable addition to the literature**. I recommend **acceptance** and encourage the authors to address the noted limitations in future work.  

**Rating:**  
**8: Accept, good paper**  

---

### Key Integrations from Alternative Reviews:
1. **Theoretical Limitations:** Added critique on linearity/MSE assumptions and limited nonlinear extension (Alt 1,2,4).  
2. **Experimental Expansions:** Incorporated need for deeper GNN validation, mixed C&S results, and baseline comparisons (Alt 1,2,3).  
3. **Practical Concerns:** Integrated scalability of deterministic variants and overfitting risks (Alt 1,3,4).  
4. **Minor Issues:** Noted notation inconsistencies (Alt 3) and suggested deeper discussion of regularization sensitivity (Alt 4).  
5. **Strengthened Context:** Highlighted the label trick’s prevalence in OGB (Alt 2) and practical significance of leakage resolution (Alt 1,4).  

*All integrated ideas are sourced exclusively from provided reviews, maintaining the original acceptance decision.*

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

### Summary of the Paper  
The authors investigate the "label trick" technique for graph neural networks (GNNs), which stochastically incorporates training labels as inputs to enable parallel propagation of features and labels. Under simplifying assumptions (linear models, MSE loss), they show this stochastic process reduces to a deterministic objective with a leakage-resistant data-fitting term and a structure-aware regularization term. The paper leverages this insight to propose novel applications: trainable label propagation, deterministic variants for linear GNNs (e.g., SGC, TWIRLS), and end-to-end trainable Correct & Smooth (C&S). Experiments on Cora, PubMed, and OGB datasets validate the theoretical insights and demonstrate performance gains.  

### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)  
**(+) Important Problem & Theoretical Foundation**: The work addresses a fundamental gap by providing theoretical grounding for the empirically successful but heuristic label trick. The derivation of an interpretable deterministic equivalent (e.g., Equation 10) revealing implicit regularization and leakage mitigation is a key contribution. This analysis meaningfully connects classical label propagation with modern GNNs.  

**(+) Novel Applications**: The proposed extensions—trainable label propagation, deterministic GNN variants, and end-to-end trainable C&S—are well-motivated by the theory. Experiments confirm their efficacy, showing consistent gains (e.g., +1-2% accuracy on benchmarks), which is valuable given the label trick’s prevalence in top OGB submissions.  

**(–) Over-Reliance on Simplifying Assumptions**: The theoretical results heavily depend on linearity and MSE loss, limiting direct applicability to complex nonlinear GNNs. While Section 3.4 attempts a nonlinear extension, it remains underdeveloped and lacks empirical validation. *Recent works like Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf) offer complementary perspectives on label/feature propagation and should be discussed.*  

**(–) Experimental Gaps**:  
- **Scope**: Experiments focus primarily on linear/two-layer models (SGC, SIGN). Broader validation on deeper architectures is needed to generalize claims.  
- **Metrics**: For regression tasks (Table 2), R² should supplement MSE.  
- **Baselines**: Comparisons for novel applications (e.g., trainable C&S) lack state-of-the-art references (e.g., GLEM). Results for C&S are mixed (gains on Cora/PubMed but marginal on ArXiv/Products), warranting deeper analysis.  
- **Deterministic vs. Stochastic**: The deterministic variant (D) lacks comprehensive benchmarking against stochastic (S), especially for computational efficiency/stability and scalability on massive graphs (e.g., OGB-Products).  

**(–) Clarity and Presentation Issues**:  
- **Theoretical Exposition**: Key steps require elaboration (e.g., Equation 5’s expectation term, undefined Γ in Section 3.3). The derivation from Equation 5’s second-last to last term is unclear; the expectation over splits (𝔼_splits) needs rigorous definition.  
- **Notation**: Excessive subscripts and inconsistent notation (e.g., parentheses vs. square brackets in Equations 4–5) hinder readability. Remarks are fragmented, disrupting flow.  
- **Motivation Gaps**: The intuition behind post-multiplying Yₜᵣ with W (Equation 3) and trainable smoothing in C&S is unexplained.  
- **Section 4.1**: The proposed procedure’s training mechanism and final model architecture (e.g., Neumann series integration) are underspecified. References to prior work on learning propagation matrices are omitted.  

**(–) Attribution & Context**: Credit should be explicitly given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf for first proposing the label trick. The regularization term’s practical tuning, sensitivity to graph properties, and potential over-smoothing risks (e.g., Equation 9) are underexplored.  

### Questions  
1. Why is the deterministic label trick absent from Figure 1’s right plot? Which GNN backbone is used in Section 5.3?  
2. In Remark 4, why does shutting off regularization imply "no chance for overfitting"?  
3. Is the deterministic decomposition (Section 3) used in Table 3 results? If not, why?  
4. How does the deterministic variant (D) scale computationally for billion-edge graphs?  
5. Were there failure cases for the label trick (e.g., high training accuracy scenarios)?  

### Minor Issues  
- Typo: "insure" → "ensure" (Remark 2).  
- Ablation studies for proposed extensions (e.g., C&S components) are missing.  

### Summary of the Review  
The paper tackles an important problem and offers valuable theoretical insights into the label trick, revealing its implicit regularization effects. The proposed applications (trainable LPA, deterministic GNNs) are promising and empirically validated. However, the work is hampered by **significant limitations**: over-reliance on linear assumptions, insufficient nonlinear validation, experimental gaps (metrics, baselines, scalability), and clarity issues (notation, motivation, attribution). These weaknesses outweigh the strengths, rendering the paper **marginally below the acceptance threshold**. Addressing these concerns—especially theoretical generality, empirical breadth, and exposition—would strengthen future iterations.  

---  
**Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)**  
*Decision: Reject*
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

reviews.text:
**Hybrid Review**

**Summary of the Paper**  
The paper investigates the "label trick," a technique for graph-based semi-supervised learning that combines feature propagation in graph neural networks (GNNs) with label diffusion. By stochastically masking ground-truth labels during training, the method aims to prevent trivial solutions (e.g., identity mapping) while leveraging both feature and label smoothness over the graph. The authors analyze the label trick through simplified theoretical models, derive a deterministic equivalent objective under linearity assumptions, and propose extensions such as trainable label propagation, deterministic GNN variants, and end-to-end trainable Correct and Smooth (C&S). Experiments on benchmarks like Cora, PubMed, and OGB datasets validate performance improvements.

**Strengths**  
The paper provides a valuable conceptual bridge between heuristic practices and theoretical understanding. By framing the label trick as a stochastic process that reduces to a deterministic objective with leakage-resistant data fitting and graph-dependent regularization, the authors offer interpretable insights into its implicit regularization effects. The novel applications, such as trainable label propagation and deterministic variants for linear GNNs (e.g., SGC, TWIRLS), are well-motivated by the analysis and demonstrate modest but consistent empirical gains. The readability of the paper is commendable, particularly in simplifying complex ideas through linear approximations.

**Weaknesses**  
While the theoretical analysis is a step forward, it relies heavily on restrictive assumptions (linear models, MSE loss), leaving the extension to nonlinear GNNs underexplored and speculative. The experiments, though thorough, focus narrowly on shallow architectures and established benchmarks, omitting comparisons with recent baselines like GLEM and failing to address scalability concerns for large-scale graphs (e.g., OGB-Products). Crucially, the paper does not rigorously justify why the label trick outperforms alternative regularization strategies (e.g., Laplacian regularization, MixUp) or resolve contradictions in prior work. For instance, the claim that graph Laplacian methods ignore features is misleading, as feature similarity inherently informs graph construction. Additionally, technical oversights—such as inconsistent notation (e.g., undefined Γ in regularization terms), insufficient discussion of hyperparameter sensitivity, and limited ablation studies—undermine the methodological rigor. The deterministic variants, while promising, lack comprehensive benchmarking against stochastic implementations, leaving practical trade-offs (e.g., computational stability, overfitting risks) unaddressed.

**Detailed Comments**  
1. **Clarity and Rigor**: The introduction’s discussion of i.i.d. vs. graph-structured data (Page 1) is tangential and confusing. A more direct problem statement, with formal definitions of the graph (node features, adjacency matrix, label matrix), would improve coherence. Equations (1) and (3) require careful reformulation to avoid ambiguities (e.g., clarify whether \(F_{tr}\) or \(F\) is minimized, specify the role of \(P\) in propagation).  

2. **Theoretical Limitations**: The duality between stochastic label masking and deterministic regularization (Page 3) is intuitively framed but lacks empirical validation. For example, the paper does not experimentally isolate the contributions of the leakage-prevention term versus the graph regularization term in Eq. 9. Furthermore, the extension to nonlinear models (Sec 3.4) is underdeveloped, relying on vague approximations without convergence guarantees or architectural constraints.  

3. **Empirical Shortcomings**: Experiments on trainable C&S show mixed results (e.g., marginal gains on OGB-ArXiv/Products), raising questions about its generalizability. The deterministic variant’s scalability for billion-edge graphs remains unaddressed, and runtime/memory overhead comparisons are absent. Ablation studies quantifying the impact of individual components (e.g., splitting probability \(\alpha\)) would strengthen the claims.  

4. **Contextualization**: The paper overlooks critical literature, such as recent feature/label integration methods (e.g., GLEM) and nonlinear regularization techniques. Claims about resolving label leakage via Eq. 6, while elegant, are not contextualized within broader GNN challenges like over-smoothing or heterophily.  

**Conclusion**  
While the paper makes incremental progress in demystifying the label trick and proposes practical extensions, its theoretical and empirical contributions are constrained by narrow assumptions, technical gaps, and insufficient engagement with the broader literature. The analysis falls short of providing a rigorous explanation for the label trick’s superiority over alternative methods or its limitations in nonlinear settings. Though the novel applications are promising, their evaluation lacks depth and scalability analysis. This work addresses an important problem but requires substantial refinement to meet publication standards.  

**Rating: 5 (marginally below the acceptance threshold)**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

### Summary of the Paper  
The paper proposes a deterministic variant of the label trick for GNNs, using a self-exclusion operator to mitigate label leakage during propagation. Theoretical analysis derives an upper bound for this deterministic approach under cross-entropy loss (Thm 2), and novel applications are introduced, including trainable label propagation and end-to-end trainable Correct & Smooth (C&S). Experiments compare deterministic and stochastic label tricks across benchmarks (Cora, PubMed, OGB), evaluating performance, regularization effects, and scalability.  

---

### Strengths  
1. **Theoretical Foundation**: The paper provides valuable theoretical grounding for the empirically successful but heuristic *label trick*, deriving a deterministic equivalent that reveals implicit regularization and leakage mitigation under linearity and MSE assumptions. This is a significant step toward interpretability.  
2. **Novel Extensions**: The proposed applications—trainable label propagation, deterministic variants for linear GNNs (e.g., SGC, TWIRLS), and end-to-end trainable C&S—are well-motivated by the analysis. The deterministic framework offers a fresh perspective on label-feature integration.  
3. **Empirical Validation**: Experiments consistently demonstrate performance gains (e.g., +1-2% accuracy) for the proposed methods across multiple datasets, validating the core theoretical insights.  

---

### Weaknesses and Major Concerns  
1. **Unclear Motivation for Deterministic Approach**: The rationale for replacing stochastic label tricks with deterministic variants remains underdeveloped. While scalability for large graphs (e.g., OGB-Products) is implied, explicit computational or stability advantages over stochastic baselines are not rigorously established. The paper does not adequately address *why* determinism is preferable beyond eliminating sampling.  
2. **Insufficient Experimental Validation**:  
   - **Critical Gaps**: There is no direct comparison between the proposed deterministic label trick (D) and state-of-the-art stochastic (S) implementations. The sole exception (Fig. 1) lacks statistical rigor, and results are not contextualized with standard deviations.  
   - **Peripheral Focus**: Experiments emphasize auxiliary comparisons (e.g., trainable vs. non-trainable propagation, with/without label trick) rather than core claims. Key results (e.g., trainable C&S in Table 4) show marginal gains on larger datasets (ArXiv, Products), undermining generality.  
   - **Lack of Ablations**: The impact of the regularization term (Γ) and splitting probability (α) is underexplored, with no ablation studies for proposed extensions.  
3. **Limitations of Theoretical Analysis**:  
   - The theory relies heavily on strong assumptions (linearity, MSE loss). Extensions to nonlinear GNNs (Sec 3.4) are preliminary and lack empirical support for complex architectures.  
   - The regularization term’s practical tuning and its relationship to graph structure (e.g., connectivity, homophily) are not discussed.  
4. **Technical and Presentation Issues**:  
   - Important references on label propagation (e.g., Szlam et al., 2008) and baseline methods (e.g., GLEM) are omitted.  
   - Algorithms and notation are not self-contained (e.g., undefined Γ in Sec 3.3; unclear C&S references).  
   - Scalability of deterministic variants for billion-edge graphs is asserted but not demonstrated.  

---

### Recommendations for Improvement  
1. **Strengthen Motivation**: Clarify the deterministic approach’s advantages (e.g., stability, speed, or memory savings) with head-to-head comparisons against stochastic baselines, including runtime/overhead metrics.  
2. **Expand Experiments**:  
   - Add direct D vs. S comparisons across datasets, with statistical significance testing.  
   - Validate nonlinear extensions and include deeper GNN architectures.  
   - Provide ablations for regularization effects and trainable C&S components.  
3. **Deepen Theoretical Discussion**: Address limitations of linear assumptions explicitly and explore approximations for nonlinear losses. Discuss potential risks (e.g., over-smoothing from regularization).  
4. **Improve Presentation**: Define notation consistently, integrate missing references, and clarify the connection to established methods like C&S.  

---

### Summary of Review  
The paper offers valuable theoretical insights into the label trick and proposes novel applications. However, **the motivation for determinism is unclear, and experimental validation is insufficient**. Critical gaps include the absence of D vs. S comparisons, marginal gains in key applications (e.g., C&S), and reliance on simplifying theoretical assumptions. While the deterministic framework is promising, these weaknesses outweigh its strengths. With major revisions, this work could reach publication standards.  

**Rating: 5 (Marginally below acceptance threshold)**  
**Decision: Reject**
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.

Main Review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?

Summary Of The Review:
The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary of the Paper:**  
This paper presents a theoretical analysis demonstrating that imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts can be reduced to reinforcement learning (RL) with a stationary reward. This stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and learner policies. The work rigorously grounds existing heuristic IL methods and formally links the approach to f-divergence minimization in adversarial IL. Empirical experiments on continuous control tasks (Hopper, Ant, Walker, HalfCheetah) validate the theoretical findings, demonstrating competitive performance, particularly with limited expert data.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Significant Theoretical Contribution:** The core theoretical analysis—showing IL reduces to RL with a stationary reward under deterministic experts and that this reward minimizes total variation distance—fills a critical gap in understanding prior heuristic methods (like Wang et al., 2019). The proofs are clear, well-motivated, and provide a solid foundation for existing approaches. The connection to adversarial IL and f-divergence minimization is insightful and novel.  
2.  **Practical Relevance and Simplicity:** The reduction offers a conceptually simple and computationally efficient alternative to adversarial IL, replacing complex discriminator training with a stationary reward function. Experiments confirm this approach achieves competitive performance, especially under data-limited regimes, validating its practical utility.  
3.  **Focus on Data Efficiency:** The experiments appropriately investigate a crucial aspect of IL: the relationship between the quantity of expert data and imitation performance. This focus aligns well with the theoretical contribution.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  **Restrictive Deterministic Expert Assumption:** The theoretical analysis critically relies on the expert being deterministic (Assumption 1). While the paper clearly analyzes the failure case for stochastic experts (Sec. 4.2), this assumption significantly limits the real-world applicability of the core theoretical contribution. More discussion on potential mitigations or approximate solutions for stochastic experts is needed.  
2.  **Theory-Experiment Gap in Reward Design:** In practice, the algorithm employs a soft reward approximation (1 - min L2 distance) to overcome sparsity. While a reasonable heuristic, this continuous relaxation lacks theoretical grounding within the paper's finite MDP framework and violates the deterministic expert assumption central to the theory. The implications of this approximation on the theoretical guarantees (e.g., the total variation bound) are not addressed.  
3.  **Limited Empirical Scope and Validation:**  
    *   The SAC expert's performance appears subpar compared to levels reported in prior literature (e.g., Berkeley DeepRL homework benchmarks), raising questions about whether observed patterns (e.g., IL performance relative to expert data size) hold with stronger experts.  
    *   The impact of expert training details (e.g., action clipping vs. constraint methods in SAC vs. Gaussian policies) on IL performance, known to be significant in practice, is unexplored.  
    *   Key comparisons are missing, notably a direct comparison to the reward method proposed in Wang et al. (2019) to motivate the choice of the L2-based reward.  
    *   The theoretical bounds depend on the mixing time (τ_mix), but the empirical validation does not investigate how this constant impacts the practical tightness or utility of the bounds.  
4.  **Presentation Issues:** Some acronyms (e.g., GMMIL in Fig 1) are used without definition. Figure 2 lacks y-axis labels. Minor grammatical errors exist (e.g., "to to"). The computational efficiency advantage (runtime in Appendix C) should be highlighted in the main text.  

**Questions:**  
1.  **Reward Design Choice:** Could the authors explicitly motivate the choice of the soft L2-based reward (R'_int) over the reward structure used in Wang et al. (2019)? How does the performance of ILR compare directly to using Wang et al.'s support estimation reward?  
2.  **Deterministic Expert Assumption:** How critical is the deterministic expert assumption in practice when using the soft reward approximation? Is it feasible to differentiate between a deterministic and a highly concentrated stochastic expert from limited training data? Could the framework be extended or provide guarantees for near-deterministic experts?  
3.  **Soft Reward Implications:** Given that the soft reward violates the deterministic expert assumption, what are the theoretical implications for the guarantees of expert reward recovery and the total variation bound? Can this relaxation be theoretically justified or analyzed?  
4.  **Mixing Time and Bounds:** How sensitive are the theoretical bounds to the mixing time τ_mix? Could practical estimation of τ_mix tighten the bounds, or are they likely to be too loose for practical use?  
5.  **Scalability and Expert Strength:** How would the approach scale to environments with higher-dimensional state spaces? Would the observed trends hold if the expert policy performance matched state-of-the-art results reported elsewhere?  

**Summary of the Review:**  
This paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by rigorously demonstrating that IL under deterministic experts reduces to RL with a stationary reward that minimizes total variation distance, providing a solid foundation for prior heuristic methods and linking to adversarial IL. The core analysis is clear and well-presented. However, the work is constrained by several significant limitations: the deterministic expert assumption limits applicability; the practical use of a soft, untheorized continuous reward approximation creates a gap between theory and experiment; and the empirical evaluation, while validating core data efficiency aspects, is limited in scope regarding expert strength, training details, baseline comparisons, and validation of bound dependencies (e.g., mixing time). Presentation issues also need attention. Despite these flaws, the theoretical grounding offered is sufficiently novel and insightful to be of interest to the community. Addressing the questions above, particularly concerning the reward approximation and expanding the empirical validation, would significantly strengthen the work.

**Rating:**  
**6: marginally above the acceptance threshold**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
In this paper, the authors show that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning can be done by reduction
to reinforcement learning with a stationary reward. theoretical analysis both
certifies the recovery of expert reward and bounds the total variation distance
between the expert and the imitation learner, showing a link to adversarial imitation
learning. Experiments are given to confirm that the reduction works well in
practice for continuous control tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The paper includes potentially interesting results. Some comments that can improve the paper are given below. 
1) In this paper, average rewards are considered. Is there any reason to consider the average reward MDP instead of discounted MDP, which is more widely used?
2) It is not clear what the norm || ||_{TV} means.
3) It would be better to discuss what kinds of RLs can be used in Proposition 1. It is not clear in the current paper. 
4) It would be better if the definition of intrinsic reward and extrinsic reward is explained in this paper. 
5) It is not clear how the imitation learner can learn the expert policy from the intrinsic reward in section 3. 
It would be better if the process is explained in more details.
6) In the experiment, it is not clear how the comaprative analysis is done. Because the authors did not develop new algorithms, 
it would be better to explain details of the comparative analysis. 
 7) Overall, the organization of the paper can be improved further to more clearly deliver the ideas by adding more detailed backgrounds.
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
In sum, the paper seems to contain potentially interesting results. 
The overall organization and presentation can be further improved to improve the readability of the paper.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary of the Paper:**  
This paper proposes a reduction from imitation learning (IL) to reinforcement learning (RL) for deterministic experts, using a stationary intrinsic reward (1 for expert state-action pairs, 0 otherwise). Theoretical analysis certifies expert reward recovery and bounds the total variation distance between expert and imitation policies under ergodicity assumptions, linking the approach to adversarial IL. Experiments on continuous control tasks (Hopper, Ant, Walker, HalfCheetah) demonstrate competitive performance with simpler implementation versus baselines (GAIL, GMMIL, SQIL), particularly with limited expert data.  

**Main Review:**  
The paper includes **novel theoretical contributions**, rigorously justifying a prior heuristic method (Wang et al., 2019) and providing non-trivial guarantees for deterministic experts. The simplicity of replacing adversarial training with a stationary reward is a practical strength, and experiments confirm data efficiency. However, several issues limit the work’s impact and clarity:  

1.  **Scope and Assumptions:** The analysis relies critically on Assumption 1 (deterministic experts), which severely restricts real-world applicability. While Sec 4.2 analyzes failure for stochastic experts, mitigation strategies are underexplored. Additionally, average-reward MDPs are used, but no justification is provided for this choice over the more common discounted setting. Extending guarantees to discounted MDPs could broaden relevance.  

2.  **Theoretical Clarity:** Key definitions require elaboration:  
    - The norm $||\cdot||_{TV}$ (total variation) is not formally defined.  
    - Proposition 1 should explicitly discuss *which RL solvers* are compatible (e.g., sample complexity assumptions).  
    - The distinction between *intrinsic* (algorithm-defined) and *extrinsic* (environment) rewards needs clarification in the main text.  

3.  **Methodology Gaps:** Section 3 lacks detail on *how* the imitation learner uses the intrinsic reward to recover the expert policy. The process (e.g., policy extraction via RL) warrants a clearer, step-by-step explanation.  

4.  **Theory-Experiment Misalignment:** Experiments employ a continuous L2-based reward relaxation ($R_{\text{int}}^{\epsilon}$) not covered by the theoretical analysis (which assumes finite MDPs). This gap is significant and unaddressed—no ablation or sensitivity analysis validates this approximation. **Theoretical grounding for this relaxation is absent.**  

5.  **Experimental Presentation:** The comparative analysis is underspecified:  
    - Details of baseline implementations (GAIL, GMMIL, SQIL) and metrics are sparse.  
    - Figure 2 lacks y-axis labels, and acronyms (e.g., GMMIL) are undefined.  
    - Computational advantages (runtime in Appendix C) should be highlighted in the main text.  
    - Including results tables would improve quantitative clarity.  

6.  **Bound Practicality:** While the total variation bound is novel, its dependence on mixing time ($\tau_{\text{mix}}$) may yield loose guarantees. Practical implications for large $\tau_{\text{mix}}$ and the feasibility of estimating $\tau_{\text{mix}}$ are unexplored.  

7.  **Organization and Readability:** Background on IL/RL (especially adversarial IL links) and core assumptions (ergodicity, determinism) could be expanded to improve accessibility. Minor grammatical errors (e.g., "comaprative," "to to") and figure issues should be corrected.  

**Summary and Rating:**  
This work makes a **valuable theoretical contribution** by certifying IL-to-RL reduction for deterministic experts, offering rigor where prior methods were heuristic. The empirical validation is solid, demonstrating simplicity and data efficiency. However, **critical limitations**—most notably the restrictive deterministic assumption, the theory-experiment gap in reward design, and loose/practicality-unclear bounds—significantly temper its impact. Presentational issues further reduce clarity. While the core idea is promising, these flaws prevent the paper from reaching its full potential. With revisions addressing these concerns (especially extending scope, aligning theory/experiments, and improving exposition), the contribution could be strengthened.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The paper presents a theoretically interesting but narrowly scoped contribution. It is accepted provisionally, pending revisions that address the major concerns above to enhance applicability, rigor, and readability.
