reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors propose a different formulation for a graph neural network, focused on achieving accuracy equal to anisotropic GNNs without anisotropic mechanisms.
reviews.main_review:
This paper is a mixed bag. On one hand, I appreciate the authors' vision: take some things we've learned about GNNs (heads, aggregators), revisit first principles (isotropic GNNs), try to come up with something new that blends them. In addition, I appreciate their take on computational efficiency: they correctly seize upon the notion that a well-understood algorithmic kernel can have meaningful computational impact, and they build on its advantages (e.g., section 3.2-aggregator fusion). On the other hand, many of the conclusions leave something to be desired. The analyses and descriptions sometimes gloss over important factors, and I wish the results delivered a stronger takeaway to match the ambition. For all the talk about computation and memory efficiency, the actual numbers are not all that substantial.

Strengths:

- The authors' direct challenge to accepted folk knowledge about anisotropic GNNs is healthy and welcome. As models evolve over time, it is important for the community to revisit fundamental positions on model components. The paper opens a lot of areas for other researchers to build off it, which I see as a major strength.
- The proposed model is clearly explained and fairly easy to follow. The intrepretations are useful and provide good intuition for how to place their proposed model in the context of other current work.
- The appendices actually provide a lot of useful content, sometimes more than the paper itself. The rationale in appendix C, for instance, was far clearer than the paper's 'aggregator fusion' section in 3.2 (even without the algorithm).

Weaknesses:

- Some of the evaluation results are weak. The accuracy-normalized results in seem to demonstrate that EGC-S  is not all that much faster or smaller than GCN. It also seems to indicate that parameter-normalized results do not correlate particularly well with memory footprint across models, which weakens the argument for O(V) vs. O(E).
- Despite a nominally primary argument of the paper being about memory efficiency and computational performance, very little quantitative results support that argument. I agree that there is definitely a lot of potential for this approach to be efficient, but I wish the paper had strong support for it.

- The authors fail to disambiguate different types of memory effects. For instance, while high-watermark memory footprint (which is mostly what is measured in their experiments) is important, they overlook the effects of parallelism. This leads them, amongst other things, to dismiss sampling methods almost out of hand. The memory behavior of sampled methods is vastly different than non-sampled GNNs when dealing with distributed execution or on graphs that are substantially larger than single-device memory capacity.  Or the focus on O(V)/O(E), which glosses over the fact the average degree is small (between 2 and 13 on their chosen OGB datasets) and ignores other 'constants' on the same order. This is technically correct but misleading. None of these invalidate the authors' methods, but it's somewhat disingenuous.

Also, it very much felt like parts of the paper were aimed at different goals. The intro and background lean heavily into hardware efficiency, while other parts of the paper (S3 outside of the last paragraph; S5.1-5.3) seem to ignore it.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The authors take a different tact with their proposed GNN, which is welcome. They explain it clearly, and the paper's writing and organization are solid. The results support their claim that they can achieve competitive accuracy, but the quantitative computation and memory results are somewhat underwhelming. In my view, the contribution of this paper is its approach and challenge to convention, which feels good enough to publish, even if it left me wanting more substantive.

Main Review:

On the other hand, many of the conclusions leave something to be desired. The analyses and descriptions sometimes gloss over important factors, and I wish the results delivered a stronger takeaway to match the ambition. For all the talk about computation and memory efficiency, the actual numbers are not all that substantial.

Weaknesses:

- Some of the evaluation results are weak. The accuracy-normalized results in seem to demonstrate that EGC-S  is not all that much faster or smaller than GCN. It also seems to indicate that parameter-normalized results do not correlate particularly well with memory footprint across models, which weakens the argument for O(V) vs. O(E).

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

### Summary of the Paper  
The authors propose Efficient Graph Convolution (EGC), an isotropic graph neural network architecture that challenges the prevailing assumption that anisotropic mechanisms are necessary for state-of-the-art performance. EGC employs spatially-varying adaptive filters through learned node-wise weightings and basis matrices, achieving competitive accuracy across six benchmark datasets (ZINC, CIFAR, MolHIV, etc.) while reducing theoretical memory complexity from O(E) to O(V). The work includes spatial and spectral interpretations of the architecture, ablation studies, and emphasizes computational efficiency through techniques like aggregator fusion.  

---

### Overall Assessment  
This paper is a mixed bag with significant strengths and notable weaknesses. I appreciate the authors' vision: revisiting first principles of isotropic GNNs while incorporating insights about heads and aggregators, and their focus on computational efficiency is timely. The direct challenge to accepted folk knowledge about anisotropic GNNs is healthy and welcome, opening avenues for community discussion. However, several methodological and presentational issues undermine the impact. While the empirical results demonstrate competitive accuracy, the quantitative support for efficiency claims is inconsistent, and key limitations remain unaddressed.  

### Strengths  
1. **Challenge to Convention**: The paper compellingly questions the necessity of anisotropic mechanisms for high performance—a foundational community assumption—backed by rigorous evaluation across diverse benchmarks. This conceptual contribution encourages re-evaluation of GNN design principles.  
2. **Clarity and Practicality**: The proposed EGC architecture is clearly explained with useful spatial interpretations. The inclusion of ablation studies (e.g., head/basis trade-offs) and implementation optimizations (e.g., aggregator fusion) provides actionable insights. The PyTorch Geometric integration and open-sourced code enhance reproducibility and practical adoption.  
3. **Theoretical Efficiency**: The O(V) memory complexity (vs. O(E) in anisotropic models) presents a meaningful theoretical advantage, particularly for large-scale graphs. The appendices (e.g., Appendix C) offer valuable rationale that clarifies design choices beyond the main text.  

### Weaknesses  
1. **Insufficient Empirical Support for Efficiency Claims**: Despite the O(V) complexity argument, quantitative results are underwhelming. The accuracy-normalized results show EGC-S is not substantially faster or smaller than GCN. Parameter-normalized metrics poorly correlate with actual memory footprints, weakening the O(V) vs. O(E) narrative. Claims of 3–7× memory reduction lack hardware context (e.g., accelerator compatibility) and omit overheads like aggregator fusion.  
2. **Oversimplified Memory Analysis**: The authors conflate high-watermark memory with parallelism and sampling implications. They dismiss sampling methods without addressing their critical role in distributed execution or out-of-core graphs. The focus on O(V)/O(E) overlooks that average degree is low (2–13 on OGB datasets), making constants non-negligible—a technically correct but misleading simplification.  
3. **Unaddressed Limitations**: Key gaps persist:  
   - **Edge Features**: Integration is dismissed without experimentation, limiting applicability to real-world graphs.  
   - **Heterophily**: Evaluation on strongly heterophilous graphs (e.g., Lim *et al.* datasets) is absent, leaving theoretical expressivity concerns unresolved.  
   - **Heuristic Design**: Aggregator selection for EGC-M is vague and heuristic-driven, risking reproducibility.  
4. **Inconsistent Narrative**: The introduction emphasizes hardware efficiency, but sections like §3.2 (aggregator fusion) and §5.1–5.3 lack depth on this theme. The spectral interpretation is underdeveloped compared to spatial analysis, and failure modes (e.g., high heterophily) are inadequately discussed.  

### Recommendations for Revision  
1. **Strengthen Efficiency Evidence**: Include latency/memory plots for accuracy-normalized models and hardware-specific analysis (e.g., accelerator compatibility). Disambiguate memory types (high-watermark vs. distributed).  
2. **Address Theoretical Gaps**: Expand spectral analysis (e.g., filter visualizations), test on heterophilous graphs, and explore edge feature integration (e.g., via line graphs).  
3. **Improve Methodological Rigor**: Automate or systematize aggregator selection for EGC-M. Provide hardware details for "out-of-memory" baselines in Table 1.  
4. **Refine Narrative**: Align sections to consistently emphasize efficiency goals. Discuss scalability to billion-edge graphs and dense graphs (where E ≈ V²).  

### Summary  
The authors present a provocative challenge to GNN orthodoxy with a well-motivated isotropic architecture (EGC). The paper excels in empirical breadth and clarity of design, making it a valuable community resource. However, methodological oversights—especially regarding efficiency validation, memory dynamics, and unaddressed edge cases—prevent it from fully delivering on its ambitions. The contribution lies primarily in its conceptual challenge and architectural approach, which are sufficiently novel to warrant publication, albeit with reservations.  

**Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)**  
*Decision: Accept with major revisions to address weaknesses above.*  

---  
**Confidential Comments to AC**: While the paper’s empirical results are strong, its technical claims about efficiency require validation. The authors should substantiate O(V) advantages on billion-edge graphs and address edge features/heterophily to counter potential expressivity critiques.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper claimed they designed a new GNN architecture that achieves state-of-the-art performance with lower memory consumption and latency. More specifically, the proposed model uses memory proportional to the number of vertices in the graph $O(V)$, in contrast to competing methods which require memory proportional to the number of edges $O(E)$. The paper claimed that the new architecture enabled each vertex to have its own weight matrix, thus following a novel adaptive filtering approach. The experiments found that the proposed efficient model could achieve higher accuracy than competing approaches across six large and varied datasets against strong baselines. Moreover, the experiments demonstrated that the proposed method achieves lower latency and memory consumption for the same accuracy compared to competing approaches.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths:
1. This paper applied the idea of "basis weights" and thus effectively uses different weight matrices for different nodes.
2. This paper has provided a long section to help interpret the proposed architecture and compared it with GCN, GAT, and PNA.

### Questions:
1. Since it is noted that different aggregators might result in outputs with different means and variances, for the experiments in Table 1, did you normalize the combination weights $w$ or the different aggregated outputs?
2. I am curious how you implemented the "weighting of bases" per node, i.e., multiplication between the combination weighting coefficients $w$ (different for different nodes) and the aggregated outputs corresponding to different basis weight matrices? Is this step before or after the aggregation? Although the theoretical complexity of this step is $O(V)$, I think the choice of actual implementation might significantly affect the practical time efficiency.

### Weaknesses:
1. The major weakness of this paper, in my opinion, is that this paper seems to have misunderstood the real bottleneck to scale-up GNNs, and the proposed architecture may not be suitable for large graphs (i.e., size over a million).
   - The paper has compared with many existing approaches to solve the scalability issues of GNNs on large graphs. However, this paper did not tackle this problem. Firstly, the theoretical complexities of the proposed architecture are still $O(V)$. Thus on a graph with more than a million nodes (which happens in some node classification or link prediction benchmarks), the memory consumption is still too large for conventional GPUs. Secondly, the main experiments (Table 1) are conducted on four graph regression/classification benchmarks and only one node classification benchmark. However, the graphs in the four graph-level benchmarks are small. The experimental exploration and evidence on large graphs are insufficient.
   - For graph sampling approaches, the paper claimed that `We evaluated a variety of sampling approaches and observed that even modest sampling levels, which provide little benefit to memory or latency, cause model performance to decline noticeably.` The corresponding experimental results are shown in Table 8 in the appendix. However, all four benchmarks used in Table 8 are graph-level tasks (graph regression or classification), where the sizes of input graphs are small. For example, ZINC and MolHIV are molecule graphs, and CIFAR consists of super-pixel sampled graphs whose sizes are at most 150 (Dwivedi et al., 2020). Applying sampling strategies to these small input graphs is not reasonable, and it is not strange to observe significant performance degradation. Actually, the performance of some sampling algorithms on relatively large graphs for node classification is strong, e.g., GraphSAINT (SAGE aggregator) can outperform the "Full-batch" GraphSAGE on *ogbn-products* (see [leaderboard](https://ogb.stanford.edu/docs/leader_nodeprop/)). The quoted sentence above is not appropriate.
   - To sum up, I think the architecture proposed in the paper does not solve the scalability problem on large graphs. Thus the authors should make this point clearer in the related work and experiment sections.
2. Another potential problem, in my opinion, is the spectral interpretation in Section 4.2. The paper claims that `Our approach corresponds to learning multiple filters and computing a linear combination of the resulting filters with weights depending on the attributes of each node locally.` The paper compared two formulas: (1) the ideal linear combination of filters: $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot g_{\theta_b}(\mathbf{L})\mathbf{X}$; and (2) the filtering of proposed model (correspond to EGC-S, Eq.(2)) $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot (\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})\mathbf{X}\Theta_b$.  However, I think the latter formula is not a combination of multiple filters. There is only one filter $(\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})$ (which is left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$) and the weight matrix $\Theta_b$ right multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ should not change the relative magnitude of signals of different frequencies. It is not the same if changing $g(\mathbf{L})$ in the former formula, where the matrix left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ changes.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: This paper proposed a novel architecture to linearly combine the aggregated outputs using different weight matrices for each node. The model is memory efficient since the memory consumption is $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$. However, the proposed method did not solve the scalability problem of GNNs when applied to considerably large graphs. And it is unclear when the provided memory efficiency is necessary, and thus the proposed method is the first choice. In terms of performance, the improvements compared with PNA are also not very convincing. The significance of this paper might be limited if the efficiency/performance improvements are marginal. I would encourage the author to explore the theoretical understanding of the proposed architecture further. Currently, section 4 is well-written, but the conclusions are limited and may have some flaws. In general, I could not recommend the current manuscript for acceptance.

The paper claimed that the new architecture enabled each vertex to have its own weight matrix, thus following a novel adaptive filtering approach. The experiments found that the proposed efficient model could achieve higher accuracy than competing approaches across six large and varied datasets against strong baselines. Moreover, the experiments demonstrated that the proposed method achieves lower latency and memory consumption for the same accuracy compared to competing approaches.

Main Review:
### Strengths:
1. This paper applied the idea of "basis weights" and thus effectively uses different weight matrices for different nodes.
2. This paper has provided a long section to help interpret the proposed architecture and compared it with GCN, GAT, and PNA.

### Questions:
1. Since it is noted that different aggregators might result in outputs with different means and variances, for the experiments in Table 1, did you normalize the combination weights $w$ or the different aggregated outputs?
2. I am curious how you implemented the "weighting of bases" per node, i.e., multiplication between the combination weighting coefficients $w$ (different for different nodes) and the aggregated outputs corresponding to different basis weight matrices? Is this step before or after the aggregation? Although the theoretical complexity of this step is $O(V)$, I think the choice of actual implementation might significantly affect the practical time efficiency.

### Weaknesses:
1. The major weakness of this paper, in my opinion, is that this paper seems to have misunderstood the real bottleneck to scale-up GNNs, and the proposed architecture may not be suitable for large graphs (i.e., size over a million).
   - The paper has compared with many existing approaches to solve the scalability issues of GNNs on large graphs. However, this paper did not tackle this problem. Firstly, the theoretical complexities of the proposed architecture are still $O(V)$. Thus on a graph with more than a million nodes (which happens in some node classification or link prediction benchmarks), the memory consumption is still too large for conventional GPUs. Secondly, the main experiments (Table 1) are conducted on four graph regression/classification benchmarks and only one node classification benchmark. However, the graphs in the four graph-level benchmarks are small. The experimental exploration and evidence on large graphs are insufficient.
   - For graph sampling approaches, the paper claimed that `We evaluated a variety of sampling approaches and observed that even modest sampling levels, which provide little benefit to memory or latency, cause model performance to decline noticeably.` The corresponding experimental results are shown in Table 8 in the appendix. However, all four benchmarks used in Table 8 are graph-level tasks (graph regression or classification), where the sizes of input graphs are small. For example, ZINC and MolHIV are molecule graphs, and CIFAR consists of super-pixel sampled graphs whose sizes are at most 150 (Dwivedi et al., 2020). Applying sampling strategies to these small input graphs is not reasonable, and it is not strange to observe significant performance degradation. Actually, the performance of some sampling algorithms on relatively large graphs for node classification is strong, e.g., GraphSAINT (SAGE aggregator) can outperform the "Full-batch" GraphSAGE on *ogbn-products* (see [leaderboard](https://ogb.stanford.edu/docs/leader_nodeprop/)). The quoted sentence above is not appropriate.
   - To sum up, I think the architecture proposed in the paper does not solve the scalability problem on large graphs. Thus the authors should make this point clearer in the related work and experiment sections.
2. Another potential problem, in my opinion, is the spectral interpretation in Section 4.2. The paper claims that `Our approach corresponds to learning multiple filters and computing a linear combination of the resulting filters with weights depending on the attributes of each node locally.` The paper compared two formulas: (1) the ideal linear combination of filters: $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot g_{\theta_b}(\mathbf{L})\mathbf{X}$; and (2) the filtering of proposed model (correspond to EGC-S, Eq.(2)) $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot (\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})\mathbf{X}\Theta_b$.  However, I think the latter formula is not a combination of multiple filters. There is only one filter $(\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})$ (which is left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$) and the weight matrix $\Theta_b$ right multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ should not change the relative magnitude of signals of different frequencies. It is not the same if changing $g(\mathbf{L})$ in the former formula, where the matrix left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ changes.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a novel architecture to linearly combine the aggregated outputs using different weight matrices for each node. The model is memory efficient since the memory consumption is $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$. However, the proposed method did not solve the scalability problem of GNNs when applied to considerably large graphs. And it is unclear when the provided memory efficiency is necessary, and thus the proposed method is the first choice. In terms of performance, the improvements compared with PNA are also not very convincing. The significance of this paper might be limited if the efficiency/performance improvements are marginal. I would encourage the author to explore the theoretical understanding of the proposed architecture further. Currently, section 4 is well-written, but the conclusions are limited and may have some flaws. In general, I could not recommend the current manuscript for acceptance.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

### Summary of the Paper  
This paper challenges the prevailing assumption that anisotropic GNNs are inherently superior by proposing Efficient Graph Convolution (EGC), an isotropic architecture leveraging spatially-varying adaptive filters. EGC combines basis weight matrices with per-node combination weights, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy across six diverse benchmarks (ZINC, CIFAR, MolHIV, etc.) while reducing memory complexity from *O(E)* to *O(V)*. The authors demonstrate lower latency and memory consumption compared to anisotropic baselines (e.g., GAT, PNA) and provide spatial/spectral interpretations. However, the scalability claims for large graphs remain unsubstantiated, and the theoretical foundations require further scrutiny.  

### Strengths  
1. **Empirical Challenge to Conventional Wisdom**: The paper compellingly questions the necessity of anisotropic mechanisms through rigorous, parameter-normalized evaluations across diverse benchmarks.  
2. **Practical Efficiency Gains**: EGC achieves 3–7× lower memory consumption than *O(E)* models (e.g., PNA) and reduces latency, offering hardware-friendly sparse-matrix compatibility.  
3. **Ablation Insights**: The analysis of design choices (e.g., heads vs. bases, aggregator fusion) provides actionable guidance for practitioners.  
4. **Interpretability**: The spatial interpretation (Section 4.1) effectively contextualizes EGC against GCN, GAT, and PNA.  

### Weaknesses  
1. **Scalability Misalignment**: The paper misattributes GNN scalability bottlenecks. While EGC reduces memory to *O(V)*, this remains prohibitive for graphs with >1M nodes (e.g., *ogbn-products*). Experiments focus on small graphs (e.g., ZINC, CIFAR with |V|≤150), and claims against sampling methods (Table 8) are invalidated by testing on graph-level tasks where sampling is ill-advised. Performance on large-scale node classification is unexplored.  
2. **Theoretical Gaps**: The spectral interpretation (Section 4.2) conflates adaptive filtering with the proposed architecture. The formulation *y* = ∑*w<sub>b</sub>* ⊙ (Ď<sup>−½</sup>ÂĎ<sup>−½</sup>)XΘ<sub>b</sub>* does not equate to combining multiple filters (*g<sub>θ<sub>b</sub></sub>*(L)*), as Θ<sub>b</sub> operates post-aggregation. A deeper analysis of homophily/heterophily trade-offs is also absent.  
3. **Edge Feature Neglect**: Despite real-world relevance, edge features are unaddressed, limiting applicability.  
4. **Heuristic-Driven Design**: Aggregator selection for EGC-M is underspecified, risking reproducibility.  

### Questions  
1. **Scalability & Implementation**:  
   - How does EGC scale to billion-edge graphs (e.g., *ogbn-papers100M*) with aggressive sampling?  
   - For the per-node weighting *w*: (a) Are aggregated outputs normalized given varying means/variances across aggregators? (b) Is weighting applied pre- or post-aggregation? While complexity is *O(V)*, implementation choices may impact latency.  
2. **Expressivity Limits**: How does EGC perform on strongly heterophilous graphs (e.g., Lim *et al.* datasets) where anisotropic mechanisms are theoretically advantageous?  
3. **Edge Features**: Could line graphs or topological message passing enable edge feature integration?  

### Summary and Recommendation  
The paper presents a novel isotropic architecture (EGC) that empirically challenges anisotropic dominance, demonstrating accuracy gains and practical *O(V)* memory efficiency. However, **the core scalability claim is unsupported**: experiments on small graphs do not address large-scale bottlenecks, and the spectral interpretation contains flaws. While the efficiency gains (latency/memory) are notable, the improvements over PNA are marginal, and unresolved issues—scalability, theoretical coherence, edge feature handling, and heuristic aggregator selection—undermine significance. The work raises valuable questions about GNN design but requires substantial revisions to validate scalability, strengthen theory, and broaden applicability.  

**Rating: 3 (Reject, not good enough)**.

### Strengths:
1. This paper applied the idea of "basis weights" and thus effectively uses different weight matrices for different nodes.
2. This paper has provided a long section to help interpret the proposed architecture and compared it with GCN, GAT, and PNA.

### Questions:
1. Since it is noted that different aggregators might result in outputs with different means and variances, for the experiments in Table 1, did you normalize the combination weights $w$ or the different aggregated outputs?
2. I am curious how you implemented the "weighting of bases" per node, i.e., multiplication between the combination weighting coefficients $w$ (different for different nodes) and the aggregated outputs corresponding to different basis weight matrices? Is this step before or after the aggregation? Although the theoretical complexity of this step is $O(V)$, I think the choice of actual implementation might significantly affect the practical time efficiency.

### Weaknesses:
1. The major weakness of this paper, in my opinion, is that this paper seems to have misunderstood the real bottleneck to scale-up GNNs, and the proposed architecture may not be suitable for large graphs (i.e., size over a million).
   - The paper has compared with many existing approaches to solve the scalability issues of GNNs on large graphs. However, this paper did not tackle this problem. Firstly, the theoretical complexities of the proposed architecture are still $O(V)$. Thus on a graph with more than a million nodes (which happens in some node classification or link prediction benchmarks), the memory consumption is still too large for conventional GPUs. Secondly, the main experiments (Table 1) are conducted on four graph regression/classification benchmarks and only one node classification benchmark. However, the graphs in the four graph-level benchmarks are small. The experimental exploration and evidence on large graphs are insufficient.
   - For graph sampling approaches, the paper claimed that `We evaluated a variety of sampling approaches and observed that even modest sampling levels, which provide little benefit to memory or latency, cause model performance to decline noticeably.` The corresponding experimental results are shown in Table 8 in the appendix. However, all four benchmarks used in Table 8 are graph-level tasks (graph regression or classification), where the sizes of input graphs are small. For example, ZINC and MolHIV are molecule graphs, and CIFAR consists of super-pixel sampled graphs whose sizes are at most 150 (Dwivedi et al., 2020). Applying sampling strategies to these small input graphs is not reasonable, and it is not strange to observe significant performance degradation. Actually, the performance of some sampling algorithms on relatively large graphs for node classification is strong, e.g., GraphSAINT (SAGE aggregator) can outperform the "Full-batch" GraphSAGE on *ogbn-products* (see [leaderboard](https://ogb.stanford.edu/docs/leader_nodeprop/)). The quoted sentence above is not appropriate.
   - To sum up, I think the architecture proposed in the paper does not solve the scalability problem on large graphs. Thus the authors should make this point clearer in the related work and experiment sections.
2. Another potential problem, in my opinion, is the spectral interpretation in Section 4.2. The paper claims that `Our approach corresponds to learning multiple filters and computing a linear combination of the resulting filters with weights depending on the attributes of each node locally.` The paper compared two formulas: (1) the ideal linear combination of filters: $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot g_{\theta_b}(\mathbf{L})\mathbf{X}$; and (2) the filtering of proposed model (correspond to EGC-S, Eq.(2)) $y=\sum_{b=1}^Bw_b\odot (\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})\mathbf{X}\Theta_b$.  However, I think the latter formula is not a combination of multiple filters. There is only one filter $(\tilde{D}^{-\frac12}\tilde{A}\tilde{D}^{-\frac12})$ (which is left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$) and the weight matrix $\Theta_b$ right multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ should not change the relative magnitude of signals of different frequencies. It is not the same if changing $g(\mathbf{L})$ in the former formula, where the matrix left multiplied to $\mathbf{X}$ changes.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposed a novel architecture to linearly combine the aggregated outputs using different weight matrices for each node. The model is memory efficient since the memory consumption is $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$. However, the proposed method did not solve the scalability problem of GNNs when applied to considerably large graphs. And it is unclear when the provided memory efficiency is necessary, and thus the proposed method is the first choice. In terms of performance, the improvements compared with PNA are also not very convincing. The significance of this paper might be limited if the efficiency/performance improvements are marginal. I would encourage the author to explore the theoretical understanding of the proposed architecture further. Currently, section 4 is well-written, but the conclusions are limited and may have some flaws. In general, I could not recommend the current manuscript for acceptance.

Rating:
3: reject, not good enough

title: Distributionally Robust Fair Principal Components via Geodesic Descents

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper considers the fair PCA with distribution ally robust optimization algorithm. The problem is of great interest. The key idea of the proposed method is to consider a penalized loss function with the penalty on the fairness criterion. The distributionally robust optimization and its reformulation is developed for parameter estimation.
reviews.main_review:
The strength of the paper is the rigorous optimization framework to solve the proposed fair PCA, with a nice reformulation and theoretical property. 

The weakness of this paper is in the following folds. First, it is not very clear on the significance of the novelty. The authors cast the PCA problem as the minimization of the penalized reconstructed error. Such a formulation is widely common in the machine learning community. Because of the L2-type loss function, the use of the distributionally robust optimization is not that surprising with the known complication on the penalty part. Second, the proposed method involves the selection of tuning parameter. While the authors seem to avoid the discussion tuning parameter selection. Moreover, the numerical experiments just consider the use of fixed value of these tuning parameters, which makes reader hard to judge the quality of the numerical comparison. Third, due the use of the penalty to engage the fairness, it is not how good the proposed method enables the fairness, especially there is no criterion on the selection of the tuning parameter \lambda. Fourth, under the context of fairness, the authors also avoid the discussion on how the choice of k, the number of PCs, would affect the fairness.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper considers the fair PCA with distribution ally robust optimization algorithm. The problem is of great interest. The key idea of the proposed method is to consider a penalized loss function with the penalty on the fairness criterion. The distributionally robust optimization and its reformulation is developed for parameter estimation.

Main Review:
The strength of the paper is the rigorous optimization framework to solve the proposed fair PCA, with a nice reformulation and theoretical property. 

The weakness of this paper is in the following folds. First, it is not very clear on the significance of the novelty. The authors cast the PCA problem as the minimization of the penalized reconstructed error. Such a formulation is widely common in the machine learning community. Because of the L2-type loss function, the use of the distributionally robust optimization is not that surprising with the known complication on the penalty part. Second, the proposed method involves the selection of tuning parameter. While the authors seem to avoid the discussion tuning parameter selection. Moreover, the numerical experiments just consider the use of fixed value of these tuning parameters, which makes reader hard to judge the quality of the numerical comparison. Third, due the use of the penalty to engage the fairness, it is not how good the proposed method enables the fairness, especially there is no criterion on the selection of the tuning parameter \lambda. Fourth, under the context of fairness, the authors also avoid the discussion on how the choice of k, the number of PCs, would affect the fairness.

Summary Of The Review:
Based on the aforementioned strengths and weakness, I would consider the proposed method contain interest merits, with the conservation of the sufficiency on the methodology novelty, framework rigor, and the applicability in practice.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

### Summary of the Paper  
This paper proposes a distributionally robust fair principal component analysis (PCA) method that minimizes a penalized loss function combining total reconstruction error and fairness criteria (absolute gap in subgroup reconstruction errors). The approach employs a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set to ensure robustness against distributional uncertainties. The min-max formulation is reformulated as a finite-dimensional optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold, solved via a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm with sub-linear convergence guarantees. Experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs compared to existing fair PCA baselines.  

### Strengths  
The paper’s primary strength lies in its **rigorous optimization framework**, which integrates fairness and distributional robustness into PCA—a timely contribution given the growing importance of equitable unsupervised learning. The **reformulation of the infinite-dimensional min-max problem** into a tractable manifold optimization (Theorem 3.2) and the accompanying **convergence guarantees** for the Riemannian algorithm are theoretically sound. The **comprehensive experimental validation** across 15 datasets showcases consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in balancing fairness and reconstruction error. Additionally, the flexibility to recover standard PCA or fair PCA under extreme hyperparameters is a notable advantage.  

### Weaknesses and Critique  
Despite these strengths, several limitations warrant attention:  
1. **Novelty and Motivation**: While the integration of robustness and fairness is commendable, the core formulation—casting PCA as penalized reconstruction error minimization—is common in machine learning. The use of distributionally robust optimization (DRO) for an L2-type loss with fairness penalties feels incremental rather than groundbreaking. The paper would benefit from **clarifying the novelty boundary** relative to prior work and providing **stronger intuition for Theorem 3.2’s reformulation** (currently relegated to appendices).  
2. **Hyperparameter Sensitivity**: The method relies heavily on tuning parameters (e.g., fairness penalty λ, ambiguity set radius ε), yet their selection is inadequately addressed. Experiments use fixed values without justification or sensitivity analysis, making it difficult to assess robustness in practice. **Guidelines for selecting λ and ε** are absent, and the impact of λ on fairness efficacy remains unquantified.  
3. **Technical and Empirical Gaps**:  
   - **Scalability**: The binary sensitive attribute assumption limits applicability; the extension to multiple groups (Appendix B) lacks empirical validation. The sub-linear convergence rate may hinder scalability for high-dimensional data, yet **runtime analysis and complexity scaling** are omitted.  
   - **Baseline Comparisons**: Experiments omit comparisons to **non-robust fair PCA variants**, preventing isolation of robustness benefits.  
   - **Ambiguity Set Design**: The choice of Wasserstein divergence and settings like εₐ = α/√Nₐ lacks statistical justification.  
4. **Contextual Omissions**: The authors avoid discussing how the **number of principal components (k) impacts fairness**, a critical oversight given the trade-offs between dimensionality reduction and subgroup parity. Additionally, **societal implications of fairness choices** (e.g., error distribution trade-offs between groups) are overlooked.  

### Recommendations for Improvement  
- **Methodology**: Motivate Theorem 3.2’s reformulation intuitively in the main text; validate conditions for its applicability.  
- **Experiments**: Add sensitivity studies for λ and ε; include runtime analysis and high-dimensional scalability tests. Compare against non-robust fair PCA to quantify robustness gains. Extend validation to multi-attribute settings.  
- **Analysis**: Discuss how k influences fairness-accuracy trade-offs and justify hyperparameter choices (e.g., εₐ) statistically.  
- **Presentation**: Clarify real-world applicability in low-data regimes and address societal impacts of fairness criteria.  

### Summary of Review  
The paper presents a **theoretically solid and empirically promising** framework for robust fair PCA. Its strengths in optimization design and experimental validation are offset by concerns about novelty delineation, tuning parameter opacity, and technical gaps in scalability and baseline comparisons. While it represents a **valuable contribution to fair unsupervised learning**, these limitations constrain its immediate impact.  

### Rating  
**6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold**  
The work advances the field but requires revisions to address methodological and presentation gaps before achieving its full potential.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper studies the formulation, reformulation and algorithm for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA. The reformulation exploits techniques from distributionally robust optimization, and the algorithm is based on reimannian sub-gradient descent. The theory is tested on UCI datasets.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

1. The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear and the problem formulation is novel.

2. Results on reformulation and riemannian gradient descent render tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors.

3. Numerical experiments are based on real data set and are comprehensive.

Weakness:

1. The formulation simply combines distributioanlly robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks.

2. The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological, but just applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

3. The dimensions of variables in UCI datasets are still relatively low (<=40 according to Table 2). The results can be strengthened by testing on datasets of larger dimensions.

My only reservation is that most theory are relatively straightforward compared to existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper studies the formulation, reformulation and algorithm for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA. The reformulation exploits techniques from distributionally robust optimization, and the algorithm is based on reimannian sub-gradient descent. The theory is tested on UCI datasets.

Main Review:
Strength:

1. The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear and the problem formulation is novel.

2. Results on reformulation and riemannian gradient descent render tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors.

3. Numerical experiments are based on real data set and are comprehensive.

Weakness:

1. The formulation simply combines distributioanlly robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks.

2. The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological, but just applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

3. The dimensions of variables in UCI datasets are still relatively low (<=40 according to Table 2). The results can be strengthened by testing on datasets of larger dimensions.

Summary Of The Review:
This paper proposes a new formulation for PCA and derives a complete set of results with regard to the computational issues of the new results. My only reservation is that most theory are relatively straightforward compared to existing literature on distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review: Distributionally Robust Fairness-Aware PCA  

### Summary of the Paper  
This paper proposes a novel formulation for distributionally robust fairness-aware PCA, integrating fairness constraints with robustness against distributional uncertainties via a Wasserstein-based ambiguity set. The min-max problem is reformulated as a finite-dimensional optimization over the Stiefel manifold, solved using a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm with sub-linear convergence guarantees. Experimental validation on UCI datasets demonstrates improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs compared to existing fair PCA baselines.  

### Strengths  
1. **Clear Motivation and Novel Formulation**: The paper is well-written and accessible, with a compelling motivation for integrating fairness and distributional robustness in PCA. The unified framework generalizes prior work (e.g., recovering standard PCA or fair PCA under extreme hyperparameters).  
2. **Theoretical and Algorithmic Soundness**: The reformulation techniques (leveraging distributionally robust optimization) and Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm are technically sound, with convergence guarantees provided. No major technical errors were identified.  
3. **Comprehensive Empirical Validation**: Experiments across 15 real-world datasets are thorough and demonstrate consistent improvements in fairness-robustness trade-offs over state-of-the-art baselines. The use of real data strengthens practical relevance.  

### Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. **Limited Methodological Originality**: While the formulation is new, it primarily combines existing tools from distributionally robust optimization and Riemannian optimization. Theoretical derivations (e.g., Theorem 3.2) appear straightforward extensions of established results, reducing methodological novelty.  
2. **Scope and Scalability Limitations**:  
   - Experiments focus on low-dimensional data (≤40 dimensions); validation on high-dimensional datasets is absent, limiting claims of generalizability.  
   - The binary sensitive attribute assumption restricts applicability. The multi-attribute extension (Appendix B) lacks empirical validation, and scalability for non-binary attributes or high dimensions remains unaddressed.  
3. **Underdeveloped Practical Considerations**:  
   - Hyperparameter tuning (e.g., ambiguity set radius *ε*, fairness weight *λ*) is heuristic and burdensome. Sensitivity analysis for these parameters is superficial.  
   - Computational scalability concerns (e.g., wall-clock time, convergence rate in practice) are not evaluated, despite the sub-linear convergence rate posing potential bottlenecks.  
4. **Incomplete Baselines and Justification**: Comparisons lack non-robust fair PCA variants, making it difficult to isolate the benefits of distributional robustness. The choice of Wasserstein divergence over alternatives is inadequately motivated.  

### Recommendations for Improvement  
- **High-Dimensional Validation**: Test scalability on datasets with dimensions ≫100 to strengthen empirical claims.  
- **Hyperparameter and Sensitivity Analysis**: Include runtime comparisons and sensitivity studies for *ε* and *λ* to guide practitioners.  
- **Expand Fairness Scope**: Validate the multi-attribute extension empirically and discuss computational complexity for subgroup pairs.  
- **Clarify Theoretical Intuition**: Provide clearer motivation for reformulation results (e.g., Theorem 3.2) in the main text.  
- **Broader Baseline Comparisons**: Add non-robust fair PCA baselines to quantify robustness-specific gains.  

### Summary of Review  
This work offers a timely integration of fairness and distributional robustness in PCA, supported by a theoretically coherent algorithm and thorough low-dimensional experiments. However, methodological originality is modest, as it extends established tools rather than developing new ones. Practical limitations—scalability, binary attributes, and heuristic hyperparameter tuning—curtail broader impact. While the paper exceeds the acceptance threshold marginally, addressing these limitations would significantly elevate its contribution.  

**Rating: 6 (marginally above the acceptance threshold)**

Main Review:
Strength:

1. The paper is nicely written and easy to follow. The motivation is clear and the problem formulation is novel.

2. Results on reformulation and riemannian gradient descent render tractable algorithms for solving the proposed problem. I do not find major technical errors.

3. Numerical experiments are based on real data set and are comprehensive.

Weakness:

1. The formulation simply combines distributioanlly robust PCA with a fairness penalty. Although this formulation is new, it seems a natural extension of existing frameworks.

2. The theoretical results are relatively easy to derive based on existing results on distributionally robust optimization and riemannian optimization. Hence, the originality is not methodological, but just applying well-established tools to a new formulation.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper aims to improve fairness and distributional robustness in dimensionality reduction techniques. More specifically, it proposes a regularization term to principal compoenent analysis (PCA) such that the expected reconstruction errors for each groups (conditioned on a binary sensitive variable) are similar, and relaxes constraint on the empirical probability distribution by maximizing over a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. This is optimized with subgradient descent on the Stiefel manifold, and numerical experiments comparing the proposed method to previous fair PCA are conducted.
reviews.main_review:
This paper tackles an interesting problem, and is mostly well-written and easy to follow. The problem formulation and re-formulation steps are not trivial.

However, there are places in this paper that needs more explanation and justification. 

- There needs to be a discussion on the assumptions for Theorem 3.2. How restrictive are they? Were they satisfied in the experiments? For example, (i) implies that the regularization parameter is less than 0.5, which will restrict the flexibility shown in Figure 1. 
- The ambiguity set B(P) is a central part of the proposed method. What is the justification for using Wasserstein-type divergence as opposed to others? Also, the second line of equation (5) needs to be explained more carefully.
- In defining $gap_\mu(U)$, is $||U'-U||_F$ the best choice to measure the difference between two orthonormal matrices? One can think of many examples with rotations where $U$ and $U'$ span the same subspace but will have very large frobenius norm difference. A more typical choice in the literature would be $||UU^T - U' U' ^T||$. 
- Is the RHS of Theorem 4.3. necessarily decreasing with $t$? It's not obvious to me why this guarantees convergence.
- Experiments and discussions on the robustness of the proposed method are lacking, compared to the fairness aspect. Are there any comparisons to other robust PCA methods, or simulation experiments?
- In the experiments, how were the sensitive attributes chosen? Were they based on what are considered sensitive attributes in the real world, e.g. race, gender, or were they chosen randomly?
- This paper is lacking a conclusion section. It would have made it stronger if the authors mentioned how they would extend this work to non-binary sensitive attributes.
- On a very high level, it's not *as* obvious why reconstruction error should be balanced compared to, for example, in supervised learning. The toy example in Figure 1 is helpful but I'm wondering if the authors can describe a real-world example where bias in reconstruction error will be harmful - where PCA is the end result rather than a preprocessing step for supervised learning problem.

Other minor problems that can be fixed with careful editing, such as:
- Page 2: "signal process" -> signal processing
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..."
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." sentence is hard to parse.
- Page 9: "In Each dataset, ..." "other features is..." "the the generalization..."
reviews.summary_of_the_review: It's a good paper, but is missing some interpretation of its theoretical results and justifications. It is likely that I will change my recommendation after the discussion period, if the authors provide more details.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper aims to improve fairness and distributional robustness in dimensionality reduction techniques. More specifically, it proposes a regularization term to principal compoenent analysis (PCA) such that the expected reconstruction errors for each groups (conditioned on a binary sensitive variable) are similar, and relaxes constraint on the empirical probability distribution by maximizing over a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. This is optimized with subgradient descent on the Stiefel manifold, and numerical experiments comparing the proposed method to previous fair PCA are conducted.

Main Review:
This paper tackles an interesting problem, and is mostly well-written and easy to follow. The problem formulation and re-formulation steps are not trivial.

However, there are places in this paper that needs more explanation and justification. 

- There needs to be a discussion on the assumptions for Theorem 3.2. How restrictive are they? Were they satisfied in the experiments? For example, (i) implies that the regularization parameter is less than 0.5, which will restrict the flexibility shown in Figure 1. 
- The ambiguity set B(P) is a central part of the proposed method. What is the justification for using Wasserstein-type divergence as opposed to others? Also, the second line of equation (5) needs to be explained more carefully.
- In defining $gap_\mu(U)$, is $||U'-U||_F$ the best choice to measure the difference between two orthonormal matrices? One can think of many examples with rotations where $U$ and $U'$ span the same subspace but will have very large frobenius norm difference. A more typical choice in the literature would be $||UU^T - U' U' ^T||$. 
- Is the RHS of Theorem 4.3. necessarily decreasing with $t$? It's not obvious to me why this guarantees convergence.
- Experiments and discussions on the robustness of the proposed method are lacking, compared to the fairness aspect. Are there any comparisons to other robust PCA methods, or simulation experiments?
- In the experiments, how were the sensitive attributes chosen? Were they based on what are considered sensitive attributes in the real world, e.g. race, gender, or were they chosen randomly?
- This paper is lacking a conclusion section. It would have made it stronger if the authors mentioned how they would extend this work to non-binary sensitive attributes.
- On a very high level, it's not *as* obvious why reconstruction error should be balanced compared to, for example, in supervised learning. The toy example in Figure 1 is helpful but I'm wondering if the authors can describe a real-world example where bias in reconstruction error will be harmful - where PCA is the end result rather than a preprocessing step for supervised learning problem.

Other minor problems that can be fixed with careful editing, such as:
- Page 2: "signal process" -> signal processing
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..."
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." sentence is hard to parse.
- Page 9: "In Each dataset, ..." "other features is..." "the the generalization..."

Summary Of The Review:
It's a good paper, but is missing some interpretation of its theoretical results and justifications. It is likely that I will change my recommendation after the discussion period, if the authors provide more details.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary of the Paper:**  
This paper proposes a distributionally robust fair principal component analysis (PCA) method to improve fairness and robustness in dimensionality reduction. The approach introduces a regularization term that minimizes the absolute gap in expected reconstruction errors across subgroups (conditioned on a binary sensitive attribute) while hedging against distributional uncertainties via a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. The problem is reformulated as a finite-dimensional optimization over the Stiefel manifold and solved using a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm with sub-linear convergence guarantees. Numerical experiments on real datasets compare the method to existing fair PCA baselines.  

**Main Review:**  
This paper tackles an important and timely problem at the intersection of fairness and robustness in unsupervised learning. It is generally well-written, with a clear problem formulation and non-trivial reformulation steps. The novel integration of distributional robustness with fairness criteria in PCA is a strength, offering a flexible framework that generalizes prior work (e.g., recovering standard PCA or fair PCA under extreme hyperparameters). The Riemannian optimization approach and convergence analysis are technically sound contributions, and the comprehensive experiments across 15 datasets demonstrate improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs over state-of-the-art baselines.  

However, several aspects require deeper justification and clarification to strengthen the work:  
1. **Theoretical Gaps**:  
   - The assumptions underpinning Theorem 3.2 (e.g., λ < 0.5) need explicit discussion. How restrictive are these conditions? Were they satisfied in the experiments? The constraint on λ may limit flexibility, as suggested by Figure 1.  
   - The reformulation in Theorem 3.2 could be better motivated; intuition is currently buried in appendices.  
   - The convergence rate in Theorem 4.3 is sub-linear, but its empirical behavior remains unevaluated. Is the RHS necessarily decreasing with *t*? This requires clarification.  

2. **Methodological Justifications**:  
   - The ambiguity set *B(P)* is central to the robustness guarantee. Why was a Wasserstein-type divergence chosen over alternatives? A rigorous justification is missing. Additionally, the second line of Equation (5) warrants careful explanation.  
   - In defining *gapᵤ(μ)*, is *||U' - U||_F* optimal for comparing orthonormal matrices? This choice is problematic, as rotations can yield identical subspaces but large Frobenius differences. A more suitable metric (e.g., *||UUᵀ - U'U'ᵀ||*) should be considered.  
   - The binary sensitive attribute assumption limits applicability. While Appendix B extends the method to multiple groups, this lacks empirical validation. Computational scalability for non-binary attributes also demands analysis.  

3. **Experimental Shortcomings**:  
   - Robustness evaluation is underdeveloped compared to fairness analysis. How does the method compare to non-robust fair PCA variants or other robust PCA baselines? Isolating robustness benefits would strengthen claims.  
   - Critical details are omitted: How were sensitive attributes chosen (e.g., real-world attributes like race/gender vs. arbitrary features)? What guided hyperparameter selections (e.g., *εₐ = α/√Nₐ*)? Sensitivity studies for *λ* and *ε* are needed.  
   - Scalability for high-dimensional data is unexplored. Wall-clock time analysis and complexity scaling with dimensions/subgroups should be included.  

4. **Broader Context and Impact**:  
   - The motivation for balancing reconstruction error (vs. other fairness metrics) needs elaboration. Beyond the toy example (Figure 1), what real-world scenarios would suffer from biased reconstruction? For instance, could this amplify errors for minority groups in downstream tasks?  
   - Societal implications of fairness choices and hyperparameter tuning deserve discussion.  
   - A conclusion section is absent. Critical next steps—like non-binary extensions or handling non-moment-based uncertainties—should be outlined.  

**Minor Issues**:  
- Page 2: "signal process" → "signal processing"  
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..." (grammar)  
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." (sentence structure unclear)  
- Page 9: "In Each dataset...", "other features is...", "the the generalization..." (typos)  

**Summary and Rating:**  
This paper presents a promising integration of fairness and distributional robustness in PCA, supported by a theoretically grounded algorithm and empirical validation. Strengths include its unified formulation and strong baseline comparisons. However, theoretical justifications (e.g., Theorem 3.2 assumptions, ambiguity set design), experimental depth (e.g., robustness tests, scalability), and real-world motivation require significant elaboration. The binary attribute focus and unvalidated extensions further limit impact. **With revisions addressing these gaps, the work could be compelling. As it stands, I recommend a weak acceptance (rating: 6—marginally above threshold)** pending author responses during the discussion period.  

---  
**Rating:**  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold  

### Key Integrations from Alternative Reviews:  
- **Strengths**: Novelty of fairness-robustness integration (Alt1/2/4), Riemannian optimization (Alt1/2/4), empirical scope (Alt1/2/4).  
- **Weaknesses**: Binary attribute limitation (Alt1/2/4), scalability concerns (Alt1/2/4), unvalidated theorem conditions (Alt3), missing baselines (Alt2).  
- **Suggestions**: Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis (Alt1/2/4), non-binary validation (Alt1/2/4), real-world motivation (Alt4).  
- **Critiques**: Ambiguity set justification (Alt3), convergence rate evaluation (Alt2/3), and experimental rigor (Alt3) are interleaved with the main review’s concerns.  
- **Decision Consistency**: The final rating (6) aligns with the main review, balancing the alternative reviews’ praise with unresolved methodological gaps.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper aims to improve fairness and distributional robustness in dimensionality reduction techniques. More specifically, it proposes a regularization term to principal compoenent analysis (PCA) such that the expected reconstruction errors for each groups (conditioned on a binary sensitive variable) are similar, and relaxes constraint on the empirical probability distribution by maximizing over a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set. This is optimized with subgradient descent on the Stiefel manifold, and numerical experiments comparing the proposed method to previous fair PCA are conducted.

Main Review:
This paper tackles an interesting problem, and is mostly well-written and easy to follow. The problem formulation and re-formulation steps are not trivial.

However, there are places in this paper that needs more explanation and justification. 

- There needs to be a discussion on the assumptions for Theorem 3.2. How restrictive are they? Were they satisfied in the experiments? For example, (i) implies that the regularization parameter is less than 0.5, which will restrict the flexibility shown in Figure 1. 
- The ambiguity set B(P) is a central part of the proposed method. What is the justification for using Wasserstein-type divergence as opposed to others? Also, the second line of equation (5) needs to be explained more carefully.
- In defining $gap_\mu(U)$, is $||U'-U||_F$ the best choice to measure the difference between two orthonormal matrices? One can think of many examples with rotations where $U$ and $U'$ span the same subspace but will have very large frobenius norm difference. A more typical choice in the literature would be $||UU^T - U' U' ^T||$. 
- Is the RHS of Theorem 4.3. necessarily decreasing with $t$? It's not obvious to me why this guarantees convergence.
- Experiments and discussions on the robustness of the proposed method are lacking, compared to the fairness aspect. Are there any comparisons to other robust PCA methods, or simulation experiments?
- In the experiments, how were the sensitive attributes chosen? Were they based on what are considered sensitive attributes in the real world, e.g. race, gender, or were they chosen randomly?
- This paper is lacking a conclusion section. It would have made it stronger if the authors mentioned how they would extend this work to non-binary sensitive attributes.
- On a very high level, it's not *as* obvious why reconstruction error should be balanced compared to, for example, in supervised learning. The toy example in Figure 1 is helpful but I'm wondering if the authors can describe a real-world example where bias in reconstruction error will be harmful - where PCA is the end result rather than a preprocessing step for supervised learning problem.

Other minor problems that can be fixed with careful editing, such as:
- Page 2: "signal process" -> signal processing
- Page 5: "By the definition the ambiguity..."
- Page 7: "At each iteration, the point..." sentence is hard to parse.
- Page 9: "In Each dataset, ..." "other features is..." "the the generalization..."

Summary Of The Review:
It's a good paper, but is missing some interpretation of its theoretical results and justifications. It is likely that I will change my recommendation after the discussion period, if the authors provide more details.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper presents an optimization based formulation of fair principal components analysis problem with a particular focus on the robustness of the optimal models. In the case when the desired robustness is specified by Gaussian distributions, the authors show how to adapt a manifold subgradient descent to solve a non-convex reformulation of the proposed fair pca formulation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength: + Robustness criterion for linear transformation of fairness can be formulated as a single optimization problem as long as the ambiguity set is defined using a single normal distribution.
+ A smooth reformulation is presented which can be solved using standard riemannian gradient descent algorithm.

Weakness: - Generalization to multiclass sensitive attribute, and other fairness metrics is missing.
- Figure 1 illustrating different approaches is nice, although it fails to capture the distributional robustness aspect of the formulation presented in the paper.
- It is hard to follow the paper since the notations are not standard.
- Experimental results are preliminary and conclusions are not clear.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: Justification:  The technical content is interesting, although in its current form, it is hard to follow. For example, why is problem (4)  infinite dimensional? As far as I can see, the decision variables in optimization problem (4) is a d by k matrix which is finite dimensional, although the zeroth order and first order oracles maybe noisy. This is evident in their convergence analysis, where they can only bound the gradient norm of the reformulated variable U (which in its original formulation is V). The paper contains some technical insights in the formulation as a SDP which happens to have a nice connection to Riemannian geometry as illustrated. However, at times, the paper reads as if one has to refer multiple textbooks just to make sure that paper indeed is technically correct. For example, many quantities appear without definitions (\hat{p}_a is not defined), and sometimes with somewhat unclear notations (Q(dx\times da). To me, the biggest technical question that the paper fails to answer is the generalization to nonbinary features. This is important in the context of the paper's contribution because we know that standard SDP solvers do not scale to settings with big or high dimensional data. The convergence rate in this setting is also not clear since a naive extension would indicate at least polynomial slowdown. The experiments are also inconclusive since the paper focuses exclusively on the binary sensitive attribute setting, and with just one metric. While the approach here may not be directly extendable to other metrics, the paper completely misses on discussing when the chosen formulation is appropriate for practitioners.

Summary Of The Paper:
The paper presents an optimization based formulation of fair principal components analysis problem with a particular focus on the robustness of the optimal models. In the case when the desired robustness is specified by Gaussian distributions, the authors show how to adapt a manifold subgradient descent to solve a non-convex reformulation of the proposed fair pca formulation.

Main Review:
Strength: + Robustness criterion for linear transformation of fairness can be formulated as a single optimization problem as long as the ambiguity set is defined using a single normal distribution.
+ A smooth reformulation is presented which can be solved using standard riemannian gradient descent algorithm.

Weakness: - Generalization to multiclass sensitive attribute, and other fairness metrics is missing.
- Figure 1 illustrating different approaches is nice, although it fails to capture the distributional robustness aspect of the formulation presented in the paper.
- It is hard to follow the paper since the notations are not standard.
- Experimental results are preliminary and conclusions are not clear.

Summary Of The Review:
Justification:  The technical content is interesting, although in its current form, it is hard to follow. For example, why is problem (4)  infinite dimensional? As far as I can see, the decision variables in optimization problem (4) is a d by k matrix which is finite dimensional, although the zeroth order and first order oracles maybe noisy. This is evident in their convergence analysis, where they can only bound the gradient norm of the reformulated variable U (which in its original formulation is V). The paper contains some technical insights in the formulation as a SDP which happens to have a nice connection to Riemannian geometry as illustrated. However, at times, the paper reads as if one has to refer multiple textbooks just to make sure that paper indeed is technically correct. For example, many quantities appear without definitions (\hat{p}_a is not defined), and sometimes with somewhat unclear notations (Q(dx\times da). To me, the biggest technical question that the paper fails to answer is the generalization to nonbinary features. This is important in the context of the paper's contribution because we know that standard SDP solvers do not scale to settings with big or high dimensional data. The convergence rate in this setting is also not clear since a naive extension would indicate at least polynomial slowdown. The experiments are also inconclusive since the paper focuses exclusively on the binary sensitive attribute setting, and with just one metric. While the approach here may not be directly extendable to other metrics, the paper completely misses on discussing when the chosen formulation is appropriate for practitioners.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review

**Summary of the Paper:**  
This paper proposes a distributionally robust fair principal component analysis (PCA) method that minimizes a combination of total reconstruction error and the absolute gap in subgroup reconstruction errors. The approach employs a min-max formulation over distributions within a Wasserstein-type ambiguity set to ensure robustness against distributional uncertainties. The authors reformulate this infinite-dimensional problem as a finite-dimensional optimization over the Stiefel manifold and solve it via a Riemannian subgradient descent algorithm, providing sub-linear convergence guarantees. Experimental validation on real-world datasets demonstrates performance improvements over existing fair PCA baselines.

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novel Formulation:** The integration of distributional robustness with fairness criteria for PCA is innovative and addresses a timely problem in unsupervised learning. The framework flexibly recovers standard PCA or fair PCA under extreme hyperparameter settings.  
2. **Theoretical Soundness:** The reformulation of the min-max problem into a tractable Riemannian optimization (particularly under Gaussian ambiguity sets) is technically sound. The convergence analysis for the manifold subgradient descent algorithm provides a solid theoretical foundation.  
3. **Empirical Validation:** Experiments across multiple real-world datasets (15 in total) comprehensively demonstrate improved fairness-accuracy trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art baselines.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Generalizability:** The paper focuses exclusively on binary sensitive attributes. While a multiclass extension is mentioned in Appendix B, it lacks empirical validation and scalability analysis. This omission is significant given that standard SDP solvers (and naive extensions) face polynomial slowdowns in high dimensions or with multiple subgroups.  
2. **Presentation and Clarity:** The paper is challenging to follow due to non-standard notations (e.g., undefined quantities like \(\hat{p}_a\) and ambiguous terms like \(Q(dx \times da)\)). Key insights (e.g., Theorem 3.2’s reformulation) are buried in appendices, and the connection to distributional robustness is inadequately illustrated (e.g., Figure 1 fails to capture this aspect).  
3. **Experimental Limitations:**  
   - Results are preliminary and lack critical baselines (e.g., non-robust fair PCA variants to isolate robustness benefits).  
   - Hyperparameter tuning (e.g., \(\lambda\), \(\epsilon\)) is heuristic and lacks sensitivity analysis.  
   - Computational scalability for high-dimensional data or non-binary attributes is unexplored, despite the sub-linear convergence rate posing potential bottlenecks.  
   - Ambiguity set selection (e.g., \(\epsilon_a = \alpha/\sqrt{N_a}\)) and its statistical justification are insufficiently discussed.  

**Critical Questions:**  
1. How does computational complexity scale with increasing dimensions or subgroup pairs? The paper provides no runtime analysis or high-dimensional validation.  
2. Why choose a Wasserstein divergence over other ambiguity sets? The suitability of this formulation for practitioners remains underexplored.  
3. Could the fairness penalty inadvertently amplify errors for minority groups? Societal impacts of fairness choices need deeper discussion.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Rating: 5 (Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold)**  
While the paper offers a theoretically interesting integration of robustness and fairness for PCA, its current form has significant limitations. The binary attribute focus restricts applicability, key technical contributions lack clarity, and experimental validation is incomplete (e.g., no scalability tests, missing baselines). The presentation requires substantial revision to improve accessibility, and the empirical section needs rigorous expansion to support claims. Addressing these issues—particularly generalizability beyond binary attributes, clarity of notation, and comprehensive experiments—would strengthen the contribution substantially.  

---

### Key Integration Points from Alternative Reviews:  
- **Strengths:** Added points about *flexible framework* (Alt 1), *theoretical soundness* (Alt 1, 2, 4), and *comprehensive experiments* (Alt 1, 2, 4).  
- **Weaknesses:** Interleaved *scalability concerns* (Alt 1, 3, 4), *ambiguity set justification* (Alt 3), *missing baselines* (Alt 2), and *hyperparameter sensitivity* (Alt 1, 3).  
- **Questions:** Incorporated *computational scaling* (Alt 1, 4), *Wasserstein justification* (Alt 3), and *societal impact* (Alt 1).  
- **Decision:** Maintained the main review’s **5** rating by emphasizing unresolved weaknesses (generalization, clarity, experiments) despite acknowledging technical novelty.

title: Meta Learning Low Rank Covariance Factors for Energy Based Deterministic Uncertainty

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The authors explore the uncertainty calibration problem in meta-learning few-shot classification, which is a relatively new topic that was seldom discussed before. 
To solve the challenges that the few samples in few-shot learning are usually insufficient for providing reliable estimation to the covariance matrices, the authors introduce meta-learning covariance matrices parametrized by a set encoder (implemented as set-transformer). And the authors further introduce scaled energy to parameterize a logit-normal softmax distribution for improving the uncertainty calibration of the softmax classification layer.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
The overall presentation of this paper is both clear and straightforward.

The figures and equations precisely facilitate the reading. 

No noticeable typos were spotted. 

The topic that the authors selected is important and largely overlooked by the research of meta-learning and few-shot learning. The effectiveness of the proposed method is reflected by the quantitative comparisons. 

Weakness:
My major concern to this paper mainly arises from the use of an additional meta-learned set encoder to predict the task-specific covariance. 
It has been shown in previous work that some meta-learners have natural advantages in quantifying uncertainty. 

For example, relying on kernel methods, Gaussian processes based meta-learners [1,2] have demonstrated strong uncertainty calibration without leveraging any auxiliary components. 

Additionally, I might be wrong, but I'm curious that if this class-level covariance can be obtained through aggregating sample-wise information so that an additional set encoder can be removed. 
Please refer to Eq.6 to Eq. 9 of [3] for an example. Although the motivations are different, I believe there is some shared wisdom. 

[1] Deep kernel transfer in gaussian processes for few-shot learning, NeurIPS 2020

[2] Bayesian Few-Shot Classiﬁcation with One-vs-Each Pólya-Gamma Augmented Gaussian Processes, ICLR 2021

[3] Amortized Bayesian Prototype Meta-learning: A New Probabilistic Meta-learning Approach to Few-shot Image Classification, AISTAT 2021
reviews.summary_of_the_review:
Overall this paper attacks a very important problem of meta learning and few-shot learning. And considering the quantitative results, the proposed method does achieve better uncertainty calibration. 
I expect further discussion on justifying the necessity of adopting an additional set encoder to achieve this capability.
